title: .NET 11 Preview 5: Brings File-Based App Improvements, New C# Features, and a Blazor Validation Wave
gist: Microsoft's .NET 11 Preview 5 ships file-based app improvements, bundles an MCP server template into the SDK, adds three preview C# features (closed classes, unions, unsafe evolution), a Blazor client-side validation wave, and quietly tailors tooling for AI/LLM environments.

insights.claim: The .NET 11 SDK now bundles the Model Context Protocol server template so 'dotnet new mcpserver' works without a separate package, indicating Microsoft is making MCP server creation a first-class, batteries-included capability of the .NET toolchain.
insights.speaker: Almir Vuk
insights.so_what: MCP becoming a default SDK template signals the protocol's mainstreaming into enterprise platforms — supports MCP-as-standard thesis.
insights.quote: The SDK also bundles the Model Context Protocol server template, so dotnet new mcpserver works without a separate package.
insights.locator: The SDK also bundles the Model Context Protocol

insights.claim: .NET 11 Preview 5 disables ANSI output during 'dotnet test' in LLM environments, showing framework vendors are now adapting developer tooling specifically for consumption by AI agents.
insights.speaker: Almir Vuk
insights.so_what: Small but telling sign that toolchains are being re-engineered for agent consumption, not just human developers — a secular shift in DevEx design.
insights.quote: the disabling of ANSI output during dotnet test in LLM environments

insights.claim: C# in .NET 11 gains three preview features: closed classes (derivable only within the same assembly, letting the compiler verify exhaustive switch expressions), union declarations (a value type holding one of a fixed set of case types with pattern matching), and Unsafe Evolution allowing pointer types outside an unsafe context.
insights.speaker: Almir Vuk
insights.so_what: Adds functional/type-safety idioms long requested by C# developers; incremental DevEx competitiveness vs other typed languages.
insights.locator: The C# language receives three notable additions

insights.claim: ASP.NET Core's Blazor in .NET 11 adds instant client-side validation without a server round-trip for server-side-rendered forms, async validation rules such as database lookups, localizable validation messages, and statically rendered QuickGrid sorting/pagination, plus a new standalone Blazor WebAssembly dev server (the Gateway).
insights.speaker: Almir Vuk
insights.so_what: Continued heavy Blazor investment signals Microsoft pushing its web stack against JS frameworks; relevant to enterprise web-platform choices.
insights.locator: ASP.NET Core focuses heavily on Blazor

insights.claim: The .NET 11 SDK can opt into checks that warn when the installed SDK has known vulnerabilities or has reached end of life, and adds stricter container registry authentication validation, embedding supply-chain and security hygiene into the toolchain.
insights.speaker: Almir Vuk
insights.so_what: Built-in vulnerability/EOL warnings reflect the industry-wide shift toward proactive supply-chain security in dev platforms.
insights.locator: projects can opt in to checks that warn
